current_fact.fact_text: In 1964, Dallas nightclub owner Jack Ruby was convicted of murdering Lee Harvey Oswald, the man accused of assassinating President John F. Kennedy. The shooting, broadcast live on television, added yet another layer of shock, conspiracy, and unresolved questions to an already traumatic national story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

current_fact.match_reason: A politically charged killing woven into America’s fabric aligns with the raw, confrontational energy of an episode centered on protest music and systemic rage.

queue.fact_text: On March 14, 1951, United Nations forces recaptured Seoul for the second time during the Korean War, retaking a capital city that had changed hands repeatedly. The see-saw struggle over Seoul symbolized the war’s brutal stalemate and the human cost of Cold War geopolitics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: While the Korean War marked a tense Cold War front, Afrobeat later became a powerful musical language of post-colonial politics and resistance, offering a rhythmic counterpoint to militarized conflict.

queue.fact_text: In 1942, Anne Miller became the first American patient successfully treated with penicillin, after doctors at a Connecticut hospital obtained a precious experimental supply. Her recovery from a life-threatening infection heralded the antibiotic revolution that would save millions of lives.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The breakthrough use of penicillin reflects decades of trial, error, and scientific learning, harmonizing with an episode dedicated to persistence, growth, and the science of progress.

queue.fact_text: Also on March 14, 1995, Norman Thagard’s journey to the Mir space station marked not just a spaceflight, but a quiet experiment in living and working together off-world. For months he orbited Earth with Russian cosmonauts, testing the limits of human cooperation in microgravity.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A literal voyage to an orbital "destination" parallels the metaphorical trip of 'Destination Sedna', both charting long journeys into shared, unfamiliar space.

queue.fact_text: Back in 1903, Theodore Roosevelt’s decision to protect Pelican Island was driven by outrage over birds being slaughtered for the fashion trade. His small executive order would grow into the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System, a sprawling network of sanctuaries for migratory birds and other species.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: Roosevelt’s fierce protection of vulnerable species aligns with the episode’s celebration of powerful, protective feminine voices that stand up against exploitation and erasure.

queue.fact_text: In 1931, crowds packed into a Bombay cinema to hear "Alam Ara"’s songs ring out from the screen—proof that audiences were hungry not just to see stories, but to hear them. The film’s success cemented music as the beating heart of Indian popular cinema.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: The birth of India’s song-driven cinema is mirrored by an episode tracing another vocal, performance-based tradition—flamenco—as it moves from intimate origins to global stages.

queue.fact_text: Far from battlefields and laboratories, the 2017 naming of nihonium in Tokyo was also a quiet celebration of national identity expressed through science. In choosing a name drawn from "Nihon," researchers inscribed Japan directly into the periodic table’s cosmic roll call.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: An element christened 'nihonium' and a band episode titled 'The One With Many Names' both play with naming as identity—how labels carry stories, pride, and myth.

published.fact_text: In 1995, astronaut and former Marine pilot Norman Thagard quietly made history by becoming the first American to reach space aboard a Russian launch vehicle. His flight on Soyuz TM-21 to the Mir space station symbolized a dramatic shift from Cold War rivalry to orbital collaboration.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal "space shift" in US–Russian relations pairs naturally with an episode themed around reimagining emptiness and space as possibility.

published.fact_text: In 1663, German polymath Otto von Guericke completed his book "Experimenta Nova," documenting spectacular vacuum experiments and the first clear description of electrostatic repulsion. His famous Magdeburg hemispheres demo showed that teams of horses couldn’t pull apart two evacuated copper domes, stunning onlookers and reshaping physics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: Electrostatic repulsion and vacuum experiments resonate perfectly with an episode themed around an 'electric shock' of sound.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1885, Gilbert and Sullivan’s comic opera "The Mikado" premiered in London, setting its satire of British society in an imagined Japan to dodge censorship. Its catchy tunes and playful jabs at bureaucracy turned it into one of the most performed operas in history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #12 - 80's Club
episode.description: Starting with a Monday evening in Munich — a few drinks, friends, and landing in an 80s dance party. From Donna Summer's hypnotic pulse, to Depeche Mode's bright synthpop, Ken Laszlo's Italo Disco romance, and Mylène Farmer's darker theatrical world. Four songs, four corners of the 80s — connected by the machines that changed how we dance.
songs: Depeche Mode - Just Can't Get Enough

published.match_reason: Both 'The Mikado' and the 80s Club episode celebrate theatrical, catchy, and highly stylized popular music that defined their eras.

published.fact_text: In 1945, the Royal Air Force dropped the enormous "Grand Slam" bomb in combat for the first time, targeting a railway viaduct near Bielefeld, Germany. Designed as an "earthquake bomb," it was so heavy it could penetrate deep into the ground before detonating, shattering structures with shockwaves.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The episode’s title 'November Bomb' makes a playful yet apt echo of the colossal Grand Slam bomb, both marking explosive turning points in their respective worlds.

published.fact_text: On March 14, 1961, a U.S. Air Force B-52 bomber carrying nuclear weapons crashed near Yuba City, California. Multiple safety systems worked as designed, preventing a nuclear detonation and turning a potential catastrophe into a chilling near-miss.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A nuclear-armed crash underscores how much we rely on complex systems, mirroring this episode’s reflection on invisible technological dependence and the anxiety around it.

published.fact_text: In 1903, Pelican Island in Florida became the first U.S. National Wildlife Refuge, declared by President Theodore Roosevelt to protect brown pelicans from being hunted to near extinction. This tiny mangrove-covered island quietly launched a vast federal conservation network.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the refuge’s creation and this episode center on protecting and admiring wild animals in their own spaces, from Florida pelicans to Arctic orcas.

published.fact_text: When Cyclone Idai made landfall near Beira, Mozambique in 2019, it unleashed catastrophic floods across Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi. The storm became one of the deadliest cyclones recorded in the Southern Hemisphere, exposing both the power of nature and the vulnerability of coastal cities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating cyclone and an episode about rain, resilience, and weathering storms create a poignant contrast between nature’s fury and human hope.

published.fact_text: In 1988, the Johnson South Reef Skirmish in the Spratly Islands saw Chinese and Vietnamese forces clash over a tiny reef surrounded by turquoise waters. The brief but deadly naval battle showed how even remote coral outcrops can become flashpoints in global geopolitics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A naval clash over reefs and an episode recorded at sea both unfold on the ocean’s surface, juxtaposing conflict with communal, peaceful voyaging.

published.fact_text: On March 14, 1931, India’s first talking film "Alam Ara" premiered, drawing such crowds that police were called in to control the queues. Its songs and spoken dialogue launched the era of sound in Indian cinema and laid the groundwork for Bollywood’s musical storytelling tradition.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: The birth of Indian sound cinema aligns with an episode about cinematic, boundary-blurring music that feels like a film score for the mind.

published.fact_text: In 1864, Rossini’s "Petite messe solennelle" was first performed not in a cathedral, but in a private Parisian mansion by just twelve singers, two pianists, and a harmonium. The composer jokingly called it his "little solemn mass," though its music is anything but modest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A small, intimate yet "solemn" chamber mass fits the reflective, quietly spiritual mood of a jazz-infused, contemplative Endless Summer morning.

published.fact_text: In 1780, during the American Revolutionary War, Spanish forces captured Fort Charlotte at Mobile, Alabama, eliminating the last British outpost that could threaten New Orleans from the east. The victory is a reminder that the struggle for American independence stretched far beyond the original thirteen colonies.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A lesser-known southern campaign in a global war for independence pairs with an episode about changing perspectives and crossing boundaries, both geographic and mental.

published.fact_text: On this day in 2017, Tokyo hosted the naming ceremony for element 113, formally christening it "nihonium" after Japan (Nihon). It was the first time an Asian country had an element named in its honor, recognizing decades of painstaking work to create superheavy atoms that exist for just fractions of a second.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: A Japanese-named element and a Japanese aesthetic philosophy meet here: fleeting, imperfect superheavy atoms echo the beauty of impermanence and wabi-sabi.

published.fact_text: In 2008, protests and riots erupted in Lhasa and spread across Tibet, fueled by long-standing tensions over culture, autonomy, and religion. The unrest coincided with preparations for the Beijing Olympics, drawing global attention to a region often described as the "roof of the world."

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Tibetan unrest and Tuareg music both speak of mountain and desert peoples negotiating identity, resistance, and spiritual heritage on the edges of powerful states.

published.fact_text: In 1991, the Escondida mine in Chile’s Atacama Desert—one of the driest places on Earth—was officially inaugurated. It would grow into the world’s most productive copper mine, extracting a metal that quietly powers everything from wiring and electronics to renewable energy systems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A massive mine carved into the desert for conductive metals contrasts with an episode about fluid immersion; both hint at what runs beneath the surface—water, copper, and hidden currents of energy.

published.fact_text: On March 14, 1647, amid the devastation of the Thirty Years' War, Bavaria, Cologne, France, and Sweden signed the Truce of Ulm. This localized ceasefire was one of many fragile agreements that paved the way toward the broader Peace of Westphalia a year later.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A war-weary continent catching its breath through a truce mirrors the episode’s invitation to slow down, pause, and inhabit a gentler tempo.

published.fact_text: In 1980, LOT Polish Airlines Flight 007 crashed near Warsaw during its final approach, killing all 87 people on board, including a U.S. amateur boxing team. The tragedy cut short the careers of young athletes who had hoped to compete at the highest levels.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: The loss of an American boxing team is poignantly matched with an episode built around boxing, honoring the sport, its discipline, and the bodies that carry it.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1972, Sterling Airways Flight 296 crashed near Kalba in the United Arab Emirates while approaching Dubai, killing 112 people. The accident underscored how navigation and terrain awareness challenges can turn routine approaches into disasters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A tragic flight approach invites a somber, reflective pairing with an episode about injury, fragility, and slowly finding a path forward after sudden rupture.

published.fact_text: In 1964, Dallas nightclub owner Jack Ruby was convicted of murdering Lee Harvey Oswald, the man accused of assassinating President John F. Kennedy. The shooting, broadcast live on television, added yet another layer of shock, conspiracy, and unresolved questions to an already traumatic national story.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A politically charged killing woven into America’s fabric aligns with the raw, confrontational energy of an episode centered on protest music and systemic rage.
